ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Caribbean-style seafood rice. FOODON_00002678 (rice dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest parent; no specific code for Caribbean-style seafood rice exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific mixed seafood rice preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Seafood Caribbean Rice is approximately 380 g, comprising a generous portion of seasoned rice topped with mixed seafood and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fried rice with seafood (SR Legacy composite) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Caribbean-style seafood rice exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for seafood fried rice (FDC ID 1100604, 'Rice, cooked, with seafood') and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite for mixed seafood rice dishes. Caribbean spice blend contribution to macros is minor and does not materially alter the profile. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for a restaurant-prepared mixed seafood rice dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous food: white rice-based mixed dish with protein
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Caribbean-style seafood rice. GI of 64 estimated from the closest analogous food in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables: white jasmine rice-based mixed dish with seafood protein, which moderates GI slightly below plain white rice (GI ~72). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 52.3, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 21.5 × 3.8 = 81.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1596 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single serving provides approximately 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~52)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in reduced portions and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given the medium GI and high per-serving glycemic load from the white rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat; beneficial omega-3 contribution from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the seafood component provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, the high sodium content (~1596 mg per serving) and use of cooking oil require individuals with cardiovascular disease to moderate portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Caribbean Rice is a Thai-restaurant fusion dish served in Singapore that combines jasmine rice stir-fried with mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish — seasoned with a Caribbean-inspired spice blend incorporating bell peppers, onion, garlic, and aromatic spices such as allspice, paprika, and thyme, finished with soy sauce and fish sauce. The dish is characterised by a mildly spiced, savoury-sweet flavour profile with tender seafood pieces and slightly chewy rice grains. It is a hearty, protein-rich one-plate meal popular in Thai casual-dining restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 380 g serving provides approximately 562 kcal, 27 g protein, 14 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1596 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~52) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The seafood component contributes vitamin B12, zinc, and omega-3 fatty acids. This dish is suitable for halal diets but contains crustacean, mollusc, fish, and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Seafood Caribbean Rice?
Is Seafood Caribbean Rice suitable for diabetics?
What allergens are in Seafood Caribbean Rice?
What seafood is in Caribbean Rice?
How much sodium is in Seafood Caribbean Rice?
What is the GI of Seafood Caribbean Rice?
Is Seafood Caribbean Rice gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Seafood Caribbean Rice exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central seafood fried rice composites and FAO/INFOODS SEA mixed seafood rice data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC seafood fried rice composite) and FAO/INFOODS SEA mixed seafood rice data; no exact approved-source match exists for this specific dish. 2. The food_name in the input ('T1 Seafood Caribbean Rice') appears to be a menu code prefix; canonical name normalised to 'Seafood Caribbean Rice'. 3. The input description references Tom Yum soup, which is inconsistent with the food name; the description has been corrected to reflect the actual dish. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for estimation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source used for estimation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the USDA composite source for this food type. 8. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice-based mixed seafood dish from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct measurement exists. 9. Halal status assumed based on typical restaurant preparation at Saap Saap Thai; not independently verified. 10. The cuisine tag 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the input are not applicable to this rice dish and have been excluded from the output.
